data.title: AutoZone: Winter Weather Takes Results Out Of Zone
data.description:
AutoZone's (AZO) share price has been in the sweet zone over the last several years. The company's stock is up over 200% in the last five years and was up even further prior to the release of its fiscal Q2 results, which landed mixed relative to expectations.

The mixed showing was due primarily to the winter weather, which impacted topline performance.

While the company did miss on this reading, it's worth noting that AZO's Q2 is typically most subject to deviation due to the weather uncertainty. Given the unusually strong season this winter, it's not entirely surprising that AZO underperformed.

I don't believe shares in AZO scream of a value proposition, and while the pullback following results is worth diving into, I don't necessarily believe investors should be in a rush to get into a stock that is trading fairly, in my view.

AZO Stock Key Metrics

AZO stock scores poorly on valuation, as assessed by the Seeking Alpha ("SA") quants, and I can understand why. Shares currently command a forward multiple of about 25x earnings, several clicks above the historicals and well above the sector median.

Shares are more aligned to averages from a price/sales perspective but still a premium to the sector, nonetheless. The higher multiple and AZO's current market positioning are partly why I'm neutral on the stock today.

The analyst community is more bullish, though coverage is on the lighter side when considering SA analysts.

Among the Wall Street crowd, chatter is more prevalent, with 28 analysts rating the stock over the last 90 days. Furthermore, about 60% of this population have rated shares as a 'strong buy' during this period.

This is likely due to AZO's continued pricing power in the face of higher consumer prices. With many priced out of the new vehicle market, most are opting for used vehicles.

The used market is great for upfront pricing, but it comes with the cost of increased repairs and maintenance. That's where AZO comes in. It's the essential nature of maintenance that has served as the primary tailwind for AZO over these last few years, and there's little to suggest that will change in the near-medium term.

AZO Q2 Results Recap

AZO's fiscal Q2 was very much distorted by the weather, but the retailer produced solid overall results, nonetheless, in my view.

During the quarter, total sales grew 8.1% YOY, with domestic same-store sales up 3.4% and international comps up 2.5% on a constant currency basis.

On the surface, EPS declined 2.3%, but that figure includes a +$59M non-cash LIFO charge tied to higher inventory costs and tariffs. Excluding that charge, EBIT would have increased 7.2% and EPS would have been up 7.1%, a much cleaner picture of underlying performance.

The swing was the winter weather. In commentaries past and present, the management team has consistently said Q2 is the hardest quarter to forecast, and this year proved that point.

Severe storms in the final four weeks of the quarter pressured commercial customers in particular. Commercial comps still rose 9.8% for the quarter, but during the two worst storm weeks, growth slowed to just over 1%, compared to more than 12% during the rest of the quarter.

On the DIY side, comps rose 1.5%, with ticket growth north of 5% driven by mid-single-digit inflation, though traffic declined 3.6%. This likely was related to the weather as well.

Overall, the quarter was positive, though with more pronounced volatility relating to the weather.

What Is The Outlook For AZO Stock?

While Q2 was impacted by winter volatility, the forward guidance conveys an affirmation of the positive demand factors, which are expected to persist.

It's worth noting that it was observed during the call that harsh winter weather typically drives failure-related repairs that show up more meaningfully in the spring and summer months. In my view, that could provide a natural tailwind as the company moves through the back half of fiscal 2026.

From a numbers standpoint, investors should expect continued LIFO pressure. The team guided for roughly a +$60M LIFO charge in each of the remaining two quarters, which will weigh on reported margins and EPS.

However, operationally, the company expects merchandise margin benefits next quarter to help offset the mix shift toward the faster-growing commercial business.

In addition, store growth remains aggressive. The retailer plans to open 350-360 stores this fiscal year, up from 304 last year, and continues expanding its Mega-Hub footprint, targeting roughly 300 locations at full buildout.

Obviously this will require increased CAPEX, and one can expect this to approach +$1.6B this year as the company invests in stores, distribution, and technology.

Is AZO Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At the end of the day, I believe shares in AZO are trading at a high-quality multiple that values it appropriately in the current market.

Current results showed that the retailer is continuing to gain market share and that commerce is growing at a healthy clip. The DIY unit also remains supported by an aging car park and stretched consumer budgets. And management is leaning into growth with store expansion, Mega-Hubs, and supply chain investments.

At roughly 25x forward earnings, however, I believe shares already reflect a lot of what makes this story compelling. Yes, the premium is arguably deserved. AZO has proven it can compound earnings through cycles, generate strong cash flow, and aggressively buy back stock. That formula has worked exceptionally well over the last decade.

But after a 200%+ five-year run, I don't see a clear mispricing here. I don't believe the winter-driven volatility changes anything with regard to the long-term thesis, but it also doesn't create a screaming opportunity.

For investors already in, I believe it remains a steady anchor worth holding. For new investors, I'd prefer a more attractive entry point. For now, AZO looks appropriately priced, and that keeps me at a 'hold.'

data.title: Philip Morris: The Stock Appears Fully Valued Based On Its Dividends
data.description:
Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) is one of the key global players in the tobacco and smoke-free products space. I started covering the firm in April 2023 with an initial neutral rating, which I recently upgraded to a buy due to its attractive dividend growth, strength and growth potential in the smoke-free segment, and its apparent undervaluation. In my most recent writing, I estimated PM's fair value to be in the range of $140 to $173 per share, using various dividend discount models.

Since then, PM's stock price has grown by more than 15%, significantly outperforming the broader market, which remained flat over the same period. Currently, the stock is trading above $186 per share, roughly 8% higher than the higher end of my previous fair value estimate.

The aim of my article today is to see whether the sharp stock price increase that we saw in the past 2 months was really warranted by the fundamentals, and whether it is still attractive to buy the stock at the current levels, especially considering the fact that the firm recently missed analyst estimates top-line. For my discussions today, I will be using the firm's latest earnings figures, and I will also update the input parameters to my dividend discount model - if necessary - to give an updated view on the valuation.

Fundamentals

In the most recent quarter, Philip Morris performed well despite missing analyst estimates. Net revenues and shipment volume both grew compared to the same period in the prior year. Adjusted operating income expanded by as much as 4.5%, while adjusted diluted EPS increased by 9% to $1.7.

When it comes to the volume growth, you may think that the 0.1% growth is negligible. So let us zoom out a bit. The following chart shows how volume grew over the past 6 years, and what the drivers of this growth were. Smoke-free products, including HTUs, oral SFPs and E-vapor, are the drivers of growth. They went from 76 billion units sold in 2020 to 179.1 billion units sold in 2025. At the same time, traditional cigarettes are becoming less and less popular, and the number of units sold showed a meaningful decline in the same period, partially offsetting the gains generated by SFPs.

Why is sales volume important?

Currently, the macroeconomic landscape is very challenging, including poor consumer sentiment, geopolitical tensions, and tariff uncertainties. So, ideally, I would prefer investing in businesses that are relatively independent from these issues and headwinds. And PM appears to be such a business. The following chart shows consumer confidence for the past 10 years. Despite sentiment being weak and volatile since 2020, PM managed to increase the number of units sold significantly. This is what makes PM's business and the stock attractive for me.

Let us dig a bit deeper into each of the product categories now. IQOS, one of the firm's most well-known SFPs, showed a significant increase in HTU (=heat-not-burn tobacco units) adjusted IMS (=in-market sales), which is a key performance indicator for PM to gauge the demand for the product.

ZYN, which is a global leader in nicotine pouches, saw a volume increase of 36% in 2025 vs. 2024. The firm's share in the nicotine pouch segment also increased substantially compared to 2024 and 2023.

VEEV, which is the fastest-growing international E-Vapor brand, saw volumes more than double in 2025 compared to 2024. VEEV is present in 47 markets globally, and is the market leader in 8 of these. Closed pod shares over the past 2 years roughly tripled.

Last, but not least, cigarettes. While the volume is declining, the firm is still able to achieve revenue growth by using their strong pricing power and the demand inelasticity. In fiscal 2024, PM increased prices by 8.7%, while in 2025, by 7.6%. As a result, gross profit increased in 2024 by 6.8% and in 2025 by 4.4%.

Looking forward, I remain optimistic about PM's business. Revenue is expected to grow in the range of 5% to 7%, operating income in the range of 7% to 9%, and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of 7.5% to 9.5%. Considering the defensive nature of the firm and the growth potential, especially in the smoke-free space, I believe that PM could be an attractive investment candidate for both growth- and income-focused investors.

While fundamentally I like PM just as much as I liked it in December 2025, from a valuation point of view, I am a bit more concerned. In my view, we should neither overpay for growth nor for safety. And if we both PM's stock now, we might be doing both. So let us see why I am saying this.

Valuation

To assess PM's fair value, I will be using a dividend discount model, just as I did before. The firm is still generating healthy cash flows, and their net debt is also trending downward, so I expect the dividends to remain safe and sustainable.

There are two key input parameters for the dividend discount model: required rate of return and dividend growth.

When it comes to the required rate of return, I will keep using the firm's cost of equity of 6.95%, just like I did previously.

When it comes to the dividend growth, I will make three assumptions, to cater for different scenarios.

For the first case - which is the most pessimistic - I will assume a dividend growth rate of 2.5% in perpetuity, which is roughly in line with the overall growth of the U.S. economy. This estimate yields a fair value of $144 per share, roughly 20% below the current market value. I believe this case is pessimistic because the firm's revenue growth, EPS growth, EBITDA growth, and dividend rate growth historically all exceed this figure.

A more realistic scenario would be to assume 3.5% dividend growth in the coming 5 years - which is equivalent to the dividend growth rate of the past 5 years - and assume a 2.5% increase in perpetuity. This yields a fair value of $151 per share, roughly 15% below the current share price.

My most optimistic case would be to assume a 6.5% dividend growth rate for the next 5 years and 2.5% afterwards in perpetuity, leading to a fair value of $173, still slightly below the current share price.

Despite the firm being fundamentally so attractive, I believe it is fully valued now. For these reasons, I cannot be bullish from a valuation point of view.

Risks

When it comes to tobacco companies, I always have to highlight the regulatory environment as the key risk. If governments were to change the legislation towards SFPs due to health concerns, it could lead to a significant decline in the demand for PM's products. One recent example of this is India banning E-cigarettes. While PM's smoke-free products are not on the Indian market currently, it could have been an appealing new market, potentially driving significant growth in volume and revenue. PM understood this and spent years lobbying for the ban to be lifted, but it did not happen. While India is the most recent case, I believe it could happen anywhere else in the world, and investors should keep it in mind.

Rapidly changing consumer preferences is also something that we need to be aware of. While it is clearly not visible in the figures now, as volume and revenue are growing and expected to grow in the near future, over a 10-year time horizon, a lot of things can change, which could reverse this growth. New products could come on the market, which could displace SFPs, just like SFPs are now displacing combustibles.

Conclusions

Despite missing analyst estimates, PM delivered a strong Q4. Volume, revenue, operating income, and diluted EPS all increased. Net debt also kept declining. All this, despite the macroeconomic headwinds, including poor consumer sentiment readings, tariff uncertainties, and geopolitical tensions.

The firm, however, appears to be fully valued now, with little upside potential, in my view.

Risks related to the regulatory environment remain significant, especially considering India's ban on certain SFPs.

For these reasons, I downgrade PM's stock to hold.

data.title: Harbor International Compounders Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
With the U.S. now comprising roughly 65% of global equity indexes, historical parallels suggest caution. - C Worldwide Asset Management

Market in Review

Europe remained characterized by low growth rather than contraction, with the European Central Bank on hold. Japan, by contrast, moved in the opposite direction, as the Bank of Japan continued its cautious policy normalization. China is still struggling with weak domestic demand, although expectations remain for further policy support.

Global equity markets were lifted by the Federal Reserve's pivot in monetary policy, and early signs of an important market shift have emerged. After a prolonged period of narrow market leadership, there are now indications that the market is broadening beyond a small group of Artificial Intelligence winners delivering strong performance. It bodes well for active management when companies across a wider range of industries, i.e., a "broader" market, can perform.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor International Compounders Fund (Institutional Class, "Fund") returned 3.19%, underperforming the benchmark, the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index, which returned 5.05%.

A large part of the underperformance can be attributed to the Fund's lack of exposure to Western banks, while having exposure to emerging markets banks and financial services companies. In addition, stock selection within Materials and Consumers Discretionary negatively affected performance. Stock selection within Health Care and lack of exposure to Communication Services was additive to relative results during the period.

Security selection in Financials, Materials, Consumer Discretionary, and Information Technology detracted the most from relative performance, while security selection in Health Care added modest value. Although sector allocation effects are residual to our bottom-up process, they weighed on relative returns this period. Security selection in Information Technology was the largest detractor, while the lack of exposure to Materials contributed positively.

Contributors and Detractors

Prosus, RELX (RELX), and Linde (LIN) detracted the most. Although Prosus reported strong half-year results for fiscal year 2026, management flagged risks from rapid AI-driven changes in consumer behavior and intensifying competition that could pressure future growth.

RELEX reported solid revenue and earnings growth for the first nine months of 2025. During the quarter, we trimmed our position in Linde, which was purely a portfolio rebalancing and funding move for our purchase of Tesco (TSCDY). Since our initial investment in Linde, it has slightly outperformed the ACWI ex-US. We still believe it holds a strong business in an attractive industry and has a highly defensive and predictable earnings stream via long-term contracts.

Key contributors to performance in the quarter were AstraZeneca (AZN) and SSE (SSEZY). AstraZeneca reported a strong third quarter, exceeding consensus on revenue, earnings before interest and taxes, and earnings per share ("EPS"), driven by oncology, while disciplined selling, general, and administrative expenses supported earnings growth. Oral oncology momentum is expected to continue into 2026, with upcoming launches requiring limited incremental investment. Management reaffirmed confidence in achieving a mid-30% margin next year, and U.S. $80bn in revenue by 2030, despite new U.S. pricing policies.

SSE shares rose following the release of fiscal year 2030 ("FY30") strategic guidance, which addressed concerns around funding its £33bn capital expenditure plan. Of this, £21bn is expected to be funded through operating cash flow, with most spending weighted to the latter part of the decade. Management reaffirmed its commitment to maintaining net debt/earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization below 4.5x FY30 guidance implying a 9%–11% EPS compound annual growth rate, alongside a dividend yield of approximately 3.5%.

Buys and Sells

During the quarter, we purchased Contemporary AmpereX Technology (CATLY), Mercado Libre, Rheinmetall (RNMBY), Tesco, and added further to Prosus.

Contemporary AmpereX Technology raises our exposure to Chinese innovation and battery technology, which we believe will have a strong future. It is the world's largest lithium-ion battery producer, spanning cell manufacturing, battery packs, recycling, and next-generation chemistries. Its competitive advantage lies in unmatched scale, vertical integration, cost leadership, and deep partnerships with global automakers. China's investment environment has also become more stable and supportive of advanced manufacturing champions, further strengthening the company's long-term positioning.

Mercado Libre offers exposure to digital consumption and financial inclusion in a structurally underpenetrated region. E-commerce penetration remains low (14% of retail vs. 44% in China), while access to financial services is limited. Mercado Libre operates a "super-app," integrating commerce, payments, credit, logistics, and advertising, creating a data-rich ecosystem well positioned for AI-driven personalization and monetization.

Rheinmetall is emerging as Europe's de facto defense systems integrator, with a broad portfolio spanning ammunition, vehicles, sensors, and drones. Europe's rearmament, structurally higher ammunition demand, and a shift toward assured-supply procurement favor scale players with captive intellectual property and domestic capacity. Rheinmetall benefits from unmatched NATO-standard ammunition capacity, strong political alignment with Germany, and returns are improving as its product mix shifts to ammunition and high-margin systems, with rising visibility via multiyear framework contracts and cost-plus elements that help preserve downside.

We sold our positions in Novo Nordisk (NVO), Atlas Copco (ATLKY), SMC (SMCAY), Ferguson (FERG), and Diageo (DEO).

We exited our Novo Nordisk position after a long and partly successful journey since our initial purchase in November 2011. Although we meaningfully reduced the position when it was at much higher levels, we stayed invested for too long. We sold it due to weakening confidence in pricing, competition, management and board changes, and the innovation pipeline. Our latest engagement suggests limited earnings growth through 2026 and increased downside risk, prompting capital reallocation.

While Atlas Copco is a high-quality compounding industrial headwinds, tariffs, foreign exchange pressure, and elevated valuation may leave the stock treading water for some time.

Sector Overweights and Underweights

Sector weightings are a residual of our bottom-up stock selection process. As of December 31, 2025, the Fund's largest sector overweights were Industrials, Consumer Discretionary, and Health Care. Compared with the MSCI ACWI ex-US Index, the Fund was overweight Industrials, Consumer Discretionary, and Health Care, while the largest underweights were Financials, Communication Services, and Energy.

Our sector weightings shifted this quarter due to recent portfolio changes. Exposure to Industrials was reduced by 2.2%, while exposure to Consumer Discretionary increased by 2.8%. These changes were driven by bottom-up stock selection rather than a top-down view of sector attractiveness.

Outlook

Equity markets have been in a phase where the magnitude of growth has been rewarded over the sustainability of growth. Speculative behavior, index-driven flows, and concentration in a narrow set of U.S. growth stocks have led to valuation distortions and challenged active managers, including ourselves. While beating the market is difficult, we believe it remains achievable with discipline and a long-term horizon. Importantly, early signs suggest a meaningful shift in market dynamics is underway. We identify four factors of change that point to a renewed focus on fundamentals and structural growth.

First, the revival of quality stocks. After several years of derating, companies with strong balance sheets, high return on capital, durable margins, and sustainable business models are now trading at reasonable valuations. Performance in late 2025 indicates investors are beginning to rediscover long-term quality as an anchor in portfolios.

Second, the broadening of the AI theme. The initial AI boom has been narrow and capital-intensive, exposing concentration and execution risks. We expect the next phase to be broader, more realistic, and global – benefiting a wider range of companies across Europe and Asia, particularly those enabling the physical and energy infrastructure required for AI.

Third, the rise of China and India. Together representing one-third of the world's population, both countries are rapidly moving up the value chain, prioritizing technology, manufacturing, and self-sufficiency. Despite their growing economic and technological importance, they remain significantly underrepresented in global equity benchmarks – an imbalance we expect to correct over time.

Fourth, peak U.S. market dominance. With the U.S. now comprising roughly 65% of global equity indexes, historical parallels suggest caution. Indexation risks reinforcing extremes, as seen previously with Japan.

Looking at 2026, we expect these four forces to drive a market reorientation toward fundamentals, diversification, and sustainable earnings growth. We believe it will be a year of realization that earnings and quality matter, with attractively priced, high-quality companies outside of the U.S. continuing to offer strong alpha potential.

Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Real Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The economy likely slowed in the fourth quarter

U.S. gross domestic product ('GDP') growth was driven by robust household consumption and a narrowing trade deficit in the third quarter, but indicators suggested a slowdown in the fourth quarter. Survey data shows that the prolonged government shutdown dampened consumer confidence and spending. Recent trade balance improvements have supported GDP, but they likely reflect inventory accumulation from earlier in the year. As these inventories are drawn down, the trade balance should normalize, which could weigh on growth. Despite potential near-term weakness, we expect the economy to rebound in the second quarter of 2026 as fiscal and monetary stimulus begins reaching consumers and businesses.

Inflation trends lower, for now

Inflation, as measured by the U.S. Consumer Price Index ('CPI'), continued easing during the fourth quarter. However, the government shutdown caused disruptions in the data collection process that may have called its reliability into question. Looking ahead, core CPI is expected to remain below 3.0% over the coming quarters. That said, substantial monetary and fiscal stimulus entering the economy could spark a second wave of inflation later in the year.

The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) continues to cut

The recent softening in labor market data has allowed the Fed to continue lowering interest rates. We expect employment measurements, which are typically lagging indicators, to deteriorate further in the near term while core inflation remains below 3%. This could provide the Fed with justification for an additional rate cut in 2026. However, yields on U.S. 2-year Treasuries are roughly aligned with the effective federal funds rate right now, suggesting that markets do not anticipate further easing unless economic conditions weaken materially.

Credit spreads remain tight

The economy likely slowed in the fourth quarter, but credit spreads remain near historical lows across both investment-grade and high-yield corporate bond markets. Demand for new issuance is strong, supported by attractive yield levels.

Although concerns around the scale of data center financing issues have drawn attention, overall issuer quality remains high. Investor appetite for these offerings continues to be robust.

Commodities, excluding oil, trade to new highs

Precious metals continued their upward trajectory during the quarter, with silver joining gold at new all-time highs. The rally broadened to include metals that are more sensitive to industrial demand. Platinum, palladium, aluminum, copper, and tin all posted significant gains. We believe this trend could signal an economic reacceleration in 2026, potentially followed by a second wave of inflation.

Fund Review

The Carillon Chartwell Real Income Fund (CHXIX) delivered strong relative and absolute performance in the fourth quarter. The Fund's I shares ranked in the 1st percentile of its Morningstar Inflation-Protected Bond peer group, and it was that group's top-performing fund for all of 2025.* The Fund's performance was supported by allocations to precious metals and equities in the materials and energy sectors. Although the portfolio's equity exposure is capped at 15%, its allocation was instrumental in smoothing returns in a rising-rate environment. The strong correlation between interest rates and cyclical sectors such as energy and materials proved highly beneficial.

During the quarter, headwinds to relative performance came from smaller equity holdings in the consumer discretionary, healthcare, and technology sectors. And while the Fund has maintained a shorter duration than its peers and the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities ('TIPS') Index, it can hold some longer-duration TIPS to help hedge. These longer-duration bonds underperformed as the yield curve steepened during the quarter.

Top securities

Silver continued catching up to gold in the fourth quarter, and the Fund's position in the iShares Silver Trust exchange-traded fund ('ETF') rallied significantly. While we expect the price of silver to consolidate after its historic run, the precious metal recently eclipsed its previous all-time high, which was set in 1980 and revisited in 2011. We expect these past highs to act as a floor for the foreseeable future.

Investors are growing comfortable with the turnaround efforts at Teva Pharmaceuticals, accepting the company's path to earnings growth. Teva's management reiterated its peak sales target for its drug Austedo, and it has initiated two phase 3 studies for its Duvakitug product. We believe that Duvakitug, which has shown promising results in treating ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, has the potential to be a blockbuster across a handful of indications.

Taseko Mines continues to ramp up its Florence copper project in Arizona. This asset and its Gibraltar mine in British Columbia could produce a substantial amount of copper in 2026, with increased output in 2027. With copper trading at all-time highs, the company's cash flow could continue growing dramatically.

Birchcliff Energy is a natural gas exploration and production company with assets in the Montney formation of Alberta, Canada. The recent ramp up in exports from its liquid natural gas facility and cooler temperatures across the region have increased the demand for Canadian gas. Prices have rebounded from near-record lows, and we continue to view the Canadian natural gas market as an attractive hedge for global energy demand.

Intel rallied significantly during the quarter as partners started lining up for the company's semiconductor manufacturing facilities. Investments were led by the U.S. government, which has taken a significant stake in the company, and we believe that Intel's manufacturing facilities are becoming vital to U.S. national security interests. The United States needs to reduce its dependence on Taiwan's manufacturing complex, and Intel provides one of the better long-term solutions.

Bottom securities

Acadia Healthcare operates behavioral health centers across the United States. Over the past two years, it grew its bed capacity significantly and planned to continue expanding in the coming years. However, activist investors have recently pushed back on these plans, urging the company to concentrate on improving operations and ramping up recently built facilities, which often take at least 12 months to become operationally profitable. We agree with the activists' recommendations and believe that Acadia could turn cash flow positive in 2026, seeing significant operational improvement by the first half of 2027.

Camping World is a leader in the recreational vehicle ('RV') dealer space. The RV industry has faced affordability issues as higher interest rates and input costs, partially due to tariffs, have pushed average monthly payments higher for new RV purchases. We view Camping World as a consolidator in the industry that can benefit from smaller dealers facing increased operating pressures. The holding also acts as a partial hedge for an economy that may run above-trend growth while the Fed keeps short-term rates low. In such an environment, we would expect RV sales to pick up dramatically.

The press has documented Oracle's elevated capital spending on artificial intelligence ('AI') over the past year, but we feel the company's stock has finally reached levels that make it an interesting hedge against AI adoption. If AI usage were to ramp up significantly over the coming years, Oracle's investment in the infrastructure for housing large language models (LLMs) could create significant free cash flow. We do not believe the company's balance sheet is overextended, though we will continue to monitor whether its capital spending increases beyond the current 2027 projections.

Mosaic produces fertilizers and has a concentrated position of potash and phosphate operations in North America and Brazil. The potash assets have performed well, but the high-cost phosphate operations are still undergoing a turnaround effort led by a new management team. We believe this team will ultimately dispose of underperforming assets and improve the company's margins.

Coeur Mining is a gold and silver miner with operations in North America. Coeur recently proposed to acquire another gold mining company in an all-stock deal that is expected to close in the first half of 2026. The deal would be immediately accretive to the company's free cash flow, but some investors worry about the multiple paid for the assets given their shorter expected mine life. However, exploration efforts and higher precious metal prices could extend the operational profitability of the two acquired mines.

Outlook

The U.S. economy ended 2025 still recovering from the six-week U.S. government shutdown. Aside from the surge in capital spending on data centers, the fourth quarter showed few signs of growth. The housing market has stalled, unemployment is edging higher, and although inflation is trending lower, cumulative price increases since 2021 have left consumers facing affordability challenges for basic goods. Absent AI-related capital spending, it is conceivable that 2025 real GDP would have finished the year close to 1.0%, instead of near 2.0% as currently projected.

This slowdown will likely extend into early 2026, but there is genuine support on the horizon. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act passed last summer was designed to release a substantial amount of fiscal stimulus in the coming year. Consumers should see cumulative federal tax refunds rise roughly 45% year over year, injecting about $150 billion (roughly 0.5% of GDP) into the economy. Businesses may also benefit from changes to accelerated depreciation that are expected to total more than $200 billion. Lower interest rates and the Fed's resumption of asset purchases should also begin to filter through the economy, creating a more favorable environment for lending.

Taken together, these supports should strengthen and broaden year-over-year economic activity. However, the implications for inflation are less certain.

Recent CPI data has been muddied by the shutdown. The Bureau of Labor Statistics ('BLS') was closed in October; it did not collect survey data and could not publish an October CPI reading. November's data was affected by a shortened collection window. Even so, inflation pressures are clearly easing, and we expect that trend to continue into 2026. At the same time, the secular inflation forces we've highlighted in prior outlooks are likely to place a floor under how low inflation can go. If growth strengthens in the second and third quarters as we expect, a second wave of inflation could follow.

To assess near-term inflation pressures, it helps to unpack the CPI's key components. Core services account for more than 60% of CPI, and shelter is more than half of core services. Shelter is measured via tenant and landlord surveys on rents; it is less volatile than home prices and typically lags home-price turning points by about 18 months. Thus, even as housing softens, this large CPI component is unlikely to collapse.

The CPI's trailing six-month shelter measure is running near 2.5% (annualized), although October's collection issues likely depressed recent readings. Adjusting for those distortions, shelter inflation is likely below 3.0% with some room to drift lower. While there are pockets of oversupply in parts of the South, we believe shelter will find a floor above 2%, because national supply and demand remain relatively balanced. Historically, shelter inflation has only dipped below 2% during significant downturns.

Beyond shelter, core services are heavily influenced by wages. The largest subcomponents include transportation services (10.5% of core services), healthcare services (10.5%), education and communication services (8.1%), and recreation services (5.3%). Growth of average hourly earnings have recently fallen to a four-year low, near 3.5%. They may drift lower in the near term, because they typically follow the quits rate, which has reached a new low.

We expect wages to find a floor in the coming quarters, likely at or above 3.0%, before inflecting higher as the economy turns. With the working-age population shrinking relative to retirees, labor retains a secular tailwind in wage negotiations that could persist into the next decade. We view the current hiring freeze primarily as a response to economic uncertainty rather than as AI-driven displacement.

Turning to goods prices (18.3% of CPI), tariffs have been a notable driver. Household furnishings and new vehicles have contributed meaningfully to recent goods inflation, but their upward pressure should lessen as tariffs are annualized. However, as tariff effects wane, the costs for input commodities used in many goods have moved substantially higher. Copper prices rose 41% in 2025. Aluminum, steel, and tin were up 17%, 32%, and 39%, respectively. The costs are likely to flow through to the price of manufactured goods in the coming year, sustaining upward pressure on goods prices.

Food (13.5% of CPI) and energy (6.4%) are highly volatile, which is why economists often focus on the core CPI measurement that excludes them. Nonetheless, they heavily influence headline CPI and shape consumer perceptions of inflation, which can affect discretionary spending. Food prices have been mixed over the past year, trending below their 10-year average, while gasoline prices have declined sharply over the last three years amid a surge in global oil supply.

We expect the oil glut to persist into early 2026. However, if prices fall much further, high-cost producers are likely to cut output quickly while China absorbs floating seaborne inventories. Moreover, predictions of demand destruction driven by electric vehicle adoption appear to have been exaggerated. Without a recession, we believe oil could find and probe a bottom in early 2026 before moving higher — an out-of-consensus view that may surprise markets in the second half.

Putting this together, we see downward pressure on CPI in the near term, with two caveats:

1. Recent seasonal adjustments have tended to add upside pressure to January readings, and

2. Artificially low shelter readings tied to the shutdown could temporarily lift shelter in the second quarter as data collection catches up.

Even so, moderating inflation should be welcome for the Fed, and it may provide cover for an additional rate cut if the labor market remains soft. However, we believe a rate cut would be a mistake: growth should begin accelerating in the spring, and further monetary easing could amplify the second wave of inflation we anticipate later in the year.

Absolute return – The return an asset achieves over a specified period of time.

Balance of trade, trade balance – The difference between the values of a nation's imports and exports for a defined period of time.

Consolidation – A term used in technical analysis to describe when assets reverse previous gains (or losses) to stay within well-defined trading levels.

Consumer Price Index ('CPI') – Measures the change in prices paid by consumers for goods and services. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics bases the index on prices of food, clothing, shelter, transportation, doctors' and dentists' services, drugs, and other goods and services that people buy for day-to-day living.

Core CPI, the U.S. Consumer Price Index ('CPI') for All Urban Consumers: All Items Less Food & Energy – A measurement of the change in prices paid by consumers for goods and services, compiled by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, which excludes two categories (food and energy) where prices tend to swing up and down more dramatically and more often than other prices.

Correlation – The degree to which two securities move in relation to each other.

Credit spread – The difference in yield between bonds of a similar maturity but with different credit quality. The spread is measured in basis points. Tighter spreads generally suggest that economic prospects are improving and the yield difference decreases as prices rise for riskier bonds that offer higher interest payments than the government.

Credit spread tightening – The contraction of spreads in response to changes in economic conditions that cause a decline in credit risk.

Cyclical sectors – Sectors that have prices influenced by macroeconomic changes in the economy and are known for following the economy as it cycles through expansion, peak, recession, and recovery.

Demand destruction – A permanent or sustained decline in demand for goods and services as a response to persistent high prices or limited supplies.

Duration – A bond's yield, coupon, final maturity, and call features incorporated into one number, expressed in years, that indicates how price-sensitive a bond or portfolio is to changes in interest rates. Bonds with higher durations carry more risk and have higher price volatility than bonds with lower durations.

Effective interest rates – The real percentage rates, adjusted for the effects of inflation, owed in interest on a loan or other debt.

Exchange-traded fund ('ETF') – A type of security that tracks a market index, sector, commodity, or other assets, but which can be bought or sold on a stock exchange the same way a regular stock or other security can. Federal funds rate — The target interest rate set by the Federal Open Market Committee of the U.S. Federal Reserve. The target is the Fed's suggested rate for commercial banks to borrow and lend their excess reserves to each other overnight.

Fiscal stimulus – Tax collection or government spending aimed at boosting growth in a country's economy.

Gross domestic product ('GDP') – The total value of goods and services provided in an economy during a specified period, often one quarter or one year.

Headwind – A term used to describe events or market forces that hinder the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments.

High-yield bonds – Bonds that pay higher interest rates because they have lower credit ratings than investment-grade bonds. High-yield bonds have credit ratings below BBB- from Standard & Poor's or below Baa3 from Moody's.

Investment-grade bonds – Fixed-income securities rated BBB or better by Standard & Poor's or Baa or better by Moody's.

Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey ('JOLTS') – A program that produces monthly data on job openings, hires, and separations compiled by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Large language models (LLMs) – Artificial intelligence algorithms that can recognize, summarize, translate, predict, and generate text, as well as respond to questions in a conversational manner, by massively large sets of data. Large language

models learn context and meaning by tracking relationships in sequential data, such as words in a sentence.

Monetary stimulus – Policy decisions made by central banks aimed at boosting growth in a country's economy.

Morningstar Inflation-Protected Bond group – Inflation-protected bond portfolios that primarily invest primarily in debt securities and adjust their principal values in line with the rate of inflation. The U.S. Treasury is currently the largest issuer of these types of securities.

Multiple, price multiple, earnings multiple – A measure of a company's value based on the ratio of its current share price to its earnings per share. This ratio is known as the price-to-earnings ratio, or P/E.

One Big Beautiful Bill Act – An act passed and signed into law in July 2025 that raised the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, made permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, and changed a wide range of other aspects of federal tax policy, making changes to health insurance legislation, phasing out or reducing credits for clean energy production or use, and removing tax benefits for illegal immigrants.

Overweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Quits rate – The number of quits, or individuals who have left jobs, during the entire month as a percentage of employment. Reported as part of the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey ('JOLTS').

Relative return – the return an asset achieves compared to a benchmark indicator over a specific period.

Secular forces – Large-scale, ongoing changes in economies or societies that have the potential to drive broad and lasting economic, technological, social or other kinds of change.

Tailwind – An event or market force that exerts a positive influence on an investment's performance.

U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities ('TIPS') – Investments intended to provide protection against inflation. The principal of a TIPS instrument increases with inflation and decreases with deflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index. When a TIPS matures, investors are paid the adjusted principal or original principal, whichever is greater.

Yield curve – A line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity.
